stress incontinence.source: Wang et al. 2026, Health Information Science and Systems 14:21, p.8 (verbatim worked example)
stress incontinence.medical_history: 1. The 68-year-old patient experiences urinary leakage when coughing, sneezing, or during urgency. 2. She used Mirabegron for one month, with symptom improvement during treatment, but symptoms recurred within two days after she stopped the medication. 3. She underwent coronary intervention two months ago.
stress incontinence.auxiliary_examination: 1. In urinalysis, the microscopic white blood cell count was 27.7/HPF two months ago and 2.1/HPF in the most recent analysis.
stress incontinence.clinical_features: 1. The patient is a 68-year-old female who has recently experienced frequent nighttime urination and incontinence, with normal urination frequency during the day but requiring three trips at night. 2. She experiences urinary leakage when coughing, sneezing, and during urgency. 3. She used Mirabegron for one month, which improved symptoms, but they recurred after discontinuation. 4. She underwent coronary intervention two months ago. 5. Urinalysis showed a high white blood cell count of 27.7/HPF two months ago, which has since decreased to normal levels at 2.1/HPF in the most recent analysis.
stress incontinence.diagnostic_basis: 1. The patient experiences urinary leakage during coughing and sneezing, which is indicative of typical stress urinary incontinence. 2. The patient exhibits urgency and increased nighttime urination, consistent with overactive bladder, but lacks other symptoms such as frequency and dysuria. The effectiveness of Mirabegron, a medication primarily used for overactive bladder, further supports this diagnosis.
stress incontinence.differential_process: 1. The patient experiences urinary leakage during urgency without symptoms like frequency or dysuria, and shows improvement with Mirabegron, allowing us to preliminarily rule out urge incontinence. 2. Overflow incontinence is often caused by lower urinary tract obstruction, such as prostatic hyperplasia. This patient has no relevant history, and the white blood cell count in the urinalysis has returned to normal, largely excluding this possibility. 3. Lower urinary tract syndrome encompasses various symptoms like frequency, urgency, and dysuria. The patient only exhibits urgency and leakage, and responds well to Mirabegron, which does not strongly align with the characteristics of lower urinary tract syndrome.

urge incontinence.medical_history: 1. A 55-year-old patient reports a sudden, compelling urge to void followed by involuntary leakage before reaching the toilet, occurring several times per week. 2. Symptoms are not triggered by coughing, sneezing, or exertion. 3. No history of pelvic surgery, neurological disease, or urinary tract instrumentation.
urge incontinence.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urinalysis negative for leukocyte esterase, nitrites, and pyuria, excluding an active urinary tract infection. 2. Post-void residual within normal limits.
urge incontinence.clinical_features: 1. Sudden, compelling urgency immediately followed by involuntary leakage, several episodes weekly. 2. No leakage associated with coughing, sneezing, or exertion. 3. Negative urinalysis and normal post-void residual.
urge incontinence.diagnostic_basis: 1. The urge to void precedes and directly triggers the leakage episodes, distinguishing this from stress incontinence, where leakage occurs without a preceding urge. 2. A negative urinalysis makes an infectious precipitant unlikely.
urge incontinence.differential_process: 1. Stress incontinence is ruled out because leakage is not provoked by coughing, sneezing, or exertion. 2. Urinary tract infection and cystitis are excluded by a negative urinalysis and culture. 3. The symptom pattern overlaps with overactive bladder, but the presence of frank incontinence, not just urgency, supports urge incontinence as the primary diagnosis.

overflow incontinence.medical_history: 1. A 70-year-old male patient reports continuous dribbling of urine and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying over the past three months. 2. He has a long-standing history of progressive weak urinary stream and straining to void.
overflow incontinence.physical_examination: 1. Palpably distended bladder on suprapubic examination.
overflow incontinence.auxiliary_examination: 1. Post-void residual elevated at 250 mL. 2. Ultrasound shows an enlarged prostate without hydronephrosis.
overflow incontinence.clinical_features: 1. Continuous or frequent dribbling with a sensation of incomplete emptying. 2. Weak, intermittent stream with straining. 3. Elevated post-void residual and a palpably distended bladder.
overflow incontinence.diagnostic_basis: 1. The elevated post-void residual and palpably distended bladder confirm an overflow mechanism rather than a storage-phase problem. 2. The long history of progressive voiding symptoms points to bladder outlet obstruction as the underlying cause.
overflow incontinence.differential_process: 1. Stress incontinence is excluded because leakage is continuous rather than provoked by exertion. 2. The enlarged prostate on ultrasound and absence of prior urethral instrumentation favor prostatic hyperplasia over urethral stricture as the obstructive cause. 3. Normal perineal sensation and no known neurological disease make a neurogenic bladder unlikely.
overflow incontinence.final_diagnosis: overflow incontinence secondary to hyperplasia of prostate

overactive bladder.medical_history: 1. A 60-year-old patient reports urgency, daytime frequency, and nocturia (four times nightly) for the past six months. 2. No urinary leakage associated with coughing or sneezing. 3. Symptoms improved partially with a trial of Mirabegron.
overactive bladder.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urinalysis negative for infection. 2. Post-void residual within normal limits.
overactive bladder.clinical_features: 1. Urgency with daytime frequency and nocturia. 2. No stress-related leakage. 3. Partial response to a beta-3 agonist. 4. Negative urinalysis and normal post-void residual.
overactive bladder.diagnostic_basis: 1. The storage symptom triad of urgency, frequency, and nocturia, without infection or elevated residual, supports overactive bladder. 2. Partial response to Mirabegron, a medication targeted at detrusor overactivity, further supports the diagnosis.
overactive bladder.differential_process: 1. Stress incontinence is excluded by the absence of exertion-triggered leakage. 2. Urinary tract infection is excluded by a negative urinalysis. 3. The symptoms are storage-predominant without significant voiding complaints, arguing against a broader lower urinary tract syndrome diagnosis. 4. True incontinence episodes are not prominent, distinguishing this from urge incontinence.

lower urinary tract syndrome.medical_history: 1. A 65-year-old male reports a mixed picture of urgency, frequency, weak stream, hesitancy, and a sensation of incomplete emptying over the past year. 2. Symptoms have gradually worsened.
lower urinary tract syndrome.physical_examination: 1. Mildly enlarged, smooth prostate on digital rectal examination.
lower urinary tract syndrome.auxiliary_examination: 1. Uroflowmetry shows a reduced maximum flow rate. 2. Post-void residual mildly elevated.
lower urinary tract syndrome.clinical_features: 1. Combined storage symptoms (urgency, frequency) and voiding symptoms (weak stream, hesitancy, incomplete emptying). 2. Gradual, progressive course. 3. Reduced flow rate and mildly elevated post-void residual.
lower urinary tract syndrome.diagnostic_basis: 1. The combination of both storage and voiding symptoms, rather than a single symptom cluster, supports a broader lower urinary tract syndrome diagnosis. 2. Reduced uroflow and mild residual elevation indicate a component of outlet obstruction.
lower urinary tract syndrome.differential_process: 1. The smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate favors benign hyperplasia contributing to the obstructive component, rather than a discrete urethral stricture. 2. No history of urethral instrumentation or trauma argues against urethral stricture. 3. Absence of perineal pain or fever argues against prostatitis. 4. The presence of significant voiding symptoms, not just storage symptoms, argues against a pure overactive bladder diagnosis.
lower urinary tract syndrome.final_diagnosis: lower urinary tract syndrome, likely related to bladder outlet obstruction from hyperplasia of prostate

carcinoma of prostate.medical_history: 1. A 72-year-old male presents with new-onset lower back pain and progressive urinary hesitancy over two months. 2. No prior history of treatment for benign prostatic enlargement.
carcinoma of prostate.physical_examination: 1. Digital rectal examination reveals an irregular, firm, nodular prostate.
carcinoma of prostate.auxiliary_examination: 1. PSA markedly elevated at 45 ng/mL. 2. Bone scan shows findings suspicious for metastatic disease.
carcinoma of prostate.clinical_features: 1. Progressive voiding symptoms with new back pain. 2. Irregular, nodular prostate on exam. 3. Markedly elevated PSA. 4. Bone scan findings suspicious for metastasis.
carcinoma of prostate.diagnostic_basis: 1. The irregular, nodular consistency of the prostate on digital rectal examination, combined with a markedly elevated PSA disproportionate to gland size, is atypical for benign disease. 2. New bone pain with suspicious bone scan findings raises concern for metastatic spread.
carcinoma of prostate.differential_process: 1. Hyperplasia of prostate is excluded because the gland is irregular and nodular rather than smoothly enlarged, and the PSA elevation is out of proportion to typical benign enlargement. 2. Absence of fever, acute pain, or infective urinary symptoms argues against prostatitis. 3. No history of prior prostate or bladder neck surgery argues against contracture of the bladder neck.
carcinoma of prostate.final_diagnosis: carcinoma of prostate, pending biopsy confirmation, with suspected bone metastasis

hyperplasia of prostate.medical_history: 1. A 68-year-old male reports gradually worsening weak stream, hesitancy, and nocturia over three years. 2. No hematuria or back pain.
hyperplasia of prostate.physical_examination: 1. Digital rectal examination reveals a smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate.
hyperplasia of prostate.auxiliary_examination: 1. PSA mildly elevated at 3.5 ng/mL, proportional to estimated gland volume on ultrasound. 2. Post-void residual mildly elevated.
hyperplasia of prostate.clinical_features: 1. Progressive voiding symptoms over several years. 2. Smooth, symmetric prostatic enlargement. 3. PSA mildly elevated proportional to gland size. 4. No hematuria.
hyperplasia of prostate.diagnostic_basis: 1. The smooth, symmetric enlargement of the prostate and a PSA elevation proportional to gland volume are typical of benign hyperplasia rather than malignancy. 2. The slow, multi-year progression is consistent with a benign obstructive process.
hyperplasia of prostate.differential_process: 1. Carcinoma of prostate is unlikely given the smooth, not nodular, gland texture and a PSA proportional to volume rather than markedly elevated. 2. No prior prostate or bladder neck surgery argues against contracture of the bladder neck. 3. No history of urethral trauma or instrumentation argues against urethral stricture. 4. Normal neurological examination and perineal sensation argue against a neurogenic bladder.

contracture of bladder neck.medical_history: 1. A 66-year-old male reports recurrence of weak stream and straining six months after a transurethral resection of the prostate. 2. No new hematuria.
contracture of bladder neck.physical_examination: 1. Digital rectal examination shows a small, previously resected prostate.
contracture of bladder neck.auxiliary_examination: 1. Uroflowmetry shows an obstructive pattern despite the small gland size. 2. Cystoscopy demonstrates a narrowed, fibrotic bladder neck.
contracture of bladder neck.clinical_features: 1. Recurrent obstructive voiding symptoms after prior prostate surgery. 2. Small prostate on exam, inconsistent with recurrent benign enlargement. 3. Cystoscopic evidence of bladder neck narrowing.
contracture of bladder neck.diagnostic_basis: 1. The history of prior prostate resection followed by recurrent obstructive symptoms, combined with a small gland and cystoscopic evidence of a narrowed, fibrotic bladder neck, is characteristic of bladder neck contracture rather than recurrent prostatic enlargement.
contracture of bladder neck.differential_process: 1. Hyperplasia of prostate is excluded because the gland is small following prior resection, not enlarged. 2. Cystoscopy localizes the narrowing specifically to the bladder neck rather than a more distal urethral segment, arguing against a primary urethral stricture. 3. Normal neurological findings argue against a neurogenic bladder.

urethral stricture.medical_history: 1. A 45-year-old male reports a progressively weak, split urinary stream and straining over one year. 2. History of urethral catheterization after a motor vehicle accident three years ago.
urethral stricture.physical_examination: 1. No palpable prostatic abnormality; mild induration along the penile urethra.
urethral stricture.auxiliary_examination: 1. Retrograde urethrogram demonstrates a discrete narrowed segment in the bulbar urethra. 2. Post-void residual mildly elevated.
urethral stricture.clinical_features: 1. Progressive weak, split stream and straining. 2. History of prior urethral instrumentation and trauma. 3. Discrete narrowing on retrograde urethrogram.
urethral stricture.diagnostic_basis: 1. The history of prior urethral catheterization and trauma, combined with a discrete narrowed segment demonstrated on retrograde urethrogram, is characteristic of a urethral stricture rather than a more proximal or diffuse obstructive process.
urethral stricture.differential_process: 1. The prostate is not enlarged on examination, arguing against hyperplasia of prostate as the cause. 2. No history of prior prostate or bladder neck surgery argues against contracture of the bladder neck. 3. Normal neurological examination argues against a neurogenic bladder. 4. Absence of active discharge or acute dysuria argues against acute urethritis, though prior untreated urethritis may have contributed to the stricture.

neurogenic bladder.medical_history: 1. A 50-year-old patient with a known T10 spinal cord injury reports urinary retention alternating with overflow leakage. 2. Reduced perineal sensation since the injury.
neurogenic bladder.physical_examination: 1. Reduced perineal and saddle sensation. 2. Palpably distended bladder.
neurogenic bladder.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urodynamic studies show detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia. 2. No structural obstruction identified on cystoscopy.
neurogenic bladder.clinical_features: 1. Voiding dysfunction with overflow leakage in the setting of a known spinal cord injury. 2. Reduced perineal sensation. 3. Urodynamic evidence of detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia without structural obstruction.
neurogenic bladder.diagnostic_basis: 1. The known spinal cord injury, reduced perineal sensation, and urodynamic evidence of detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia without a structural lesion on cystoscopy together confirm a neurogenic cause of the voiding dysfunction.
neurogenic bladder.differential_process: 1. Cystoscopy shows no structural obstruction, excluding hyperplasia of prostate, urethral stricture, and contracture of the bladder neck as the primary cause. 2. The overflow leakage observed is a consequence of the underlying neurogenic dysfunction rather than a separate primary diagnosis.

carcinoma of bladder.medical_history: 1. A 68-year-old male smoker presents with three episodes of painless gross hematuria over the past month. 2. No associated fever or flank pain.
carcinoma of bladder.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urine cytology positive for atypical/malignant cells. 2. CT urogram shows a soft-tissue mass along the bladder wall.
carcinoma of bladder.clinical_features: 1. Painless, intermittent gross hematuria. 2. Significant smoking history. 3. Positive urine cytology. 4. Soft-tissue bladder wall mass on imaging.
carcinoma of bladder.diagnostic_basis: 1. Painless gross hematuria in an older patient with a significant smoking history is a classic presentation for bladder malignancy. 2. Positive urine cytology and a soft-tissue mass on CT urogram support the diagnosis.
carcinoma of bladder.differential_process: 1. Vesical calculus is excluded because imaging shows a soft-tissue mass rather than a calcified, mobile stone. 2. Cystitis and urinary tract infection are excluded by a negative urinalysis and culture and the absence of irritative voiding symptoms, plus the painless nature of the hematuria.
carcinoma of bladder.final_diagnosis: carcinoma of bladder, pending cystoscopic biopsy confirmation

vesical calculus.medical_history: 1. A 55-year-old male with known benign prostatic hyperplasia reports suprapubic pain that worsens toward the end of urination, with intermittent interruption of the stream. 2. Occasional terminal hematuria.
vesical calculus.auxiliary_examination: 1. Plain abdominal X-ray shows a radio-opaque density in the bladder region. 2. Ultrasound confirms a mobile, echogenic stone within the bladder.
vesical calculus.clinical_features: 1. Suprapubic pain worse at end of micturition with positional interruption of the stream. 2. Terminal hematuria. 3. Imaging-confirmed bladder stone. 4. Underlying benign prostatic hyperplasia as a predisposing factor.
vesical calculus.diagnostic_basis: 1. The positional, end-of-stream pain with stream interruption, terminal hematuria, and imaging-confirmed mobile echogenic stone are characteristic of a bladder calculus, often precipitated by underlying outlet obstruction from prostatic hyperplasia.
vesical calculus.differential_process: 1. Carcinoma of bladder is excluded because imaging shows a discrete, mobile, echogenic/radio-opaque stone rather than a fixed soft-tissue mass, and cytology is negative. 2. Cystitis is considered a possible secondary irritative contributor but not the primary diagnosis, given the imaging findings. 3. The underlying hyperplasia of prostate is noted as a predisposing rather than a competing primary diagnosis.
vesical calculus.final_diagnosis: vesical calculus secondary to bladder outlet obstruction from hyperplasia of prostate

urinary tract infection.medical_history: 1. A 30-year-old female reports acute onset of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort for two days. 2. No fever, flank pain, or vaginal discharge.
urinary tract infection.physical_examination: 1. Mild suprapubic tenderness; no costovertebral angle tenderness.
urinary tract infection.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urinalysis positive for leukocyte esterase, nitrites, and pyuria. 2. Urine culture pending.
urinary tract infection.clinical_features: 1. Acute dysuria, frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. 2. No fever or flank pain. 3. Positive urinalysis for infection markers.
urinary tract infection.diagnostic_basis: 1. The acute onset of dysuria and frequency with suprapubic discomfort, positive leukocyte esterase, nitrites, and pyuria on urinalysis, and absence of fever or flank pain, arguing against upper tract involvement, support a diagnosis of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection.
urinary tract infection.differential_process: 1. Absence of vaginal discharge or external-only dysuria argues against vaginitis. 2. Absence of urethral discharge and no recent new sexual partner argue against urethritis. 3. The patient is female, making prostatitis inapplicable. 4. The suprapubic-predominant symptoms with a positive urinalysis are most consistent with cystitis, which is closely related to and often used interchangeably with a lower urinary tract infection in this presentation.

cystitis.medical_history: 1. A 40-year-old female reports recurrent suprapubic pain, urgency, and frequency, now on her third episode this year. 2. No fever.
cystitis.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urinalysis shows pyuria and bacteriuria. 2. Urine culture grows E. coli. 3. Renal and bladder ultrasound obtained to evaluate for a predisposing structural cause given recurrence.
cystitis.clinical_features: 1. Recurrent suprapubic pain, urgency, and frequency. 2. Suprapubic tenderness. 3. Pyuria and bacteriuria confirmed on culture. 4. No fever or flank pain.
cystitis.diagnostic_basis: 1. Suprapubic pain, urgency, and frequency with confirmed pyuria and a positive urine culture, in the absence of fever or flank pain, support a diagnosis of cystitis rather than upper tract or systemic infection.
cystitis.differential_process: 1. Cystitis is considered synonymous with the presenting lower urinary tract infection in this case. 2. Given the recurrent nature, imaging is obtained to exclude a vesical calculus or bladder tumor as an underlying predisposing cause; both are excluded here by a normal ultrasound. 3. Urge incontinence is excluded because there is no involuntary leakage, only irritative voiding symptoms.

vaginitis.medical_history: 1. A 28-year-old female reports vaginal itching, discharge, and external burning with urination for one week. 2. No urinary frequency or urgency.
vaginitis.physical_examination: 1. Erythematous vaginal mucosa with discharge on speculum examination.
vaginitis.auxiliary_examination: 1. Wet mount shows clue cells or pseudohyphae, organism-dependent. 2. Urinalysis unremarkable, without pyuria or nitrites.
vaginitis.clinical_features: 1. Vaginal itching and discharge with external, not internal, dysuria. 2. No true urinary frequency or urgency. 3. Erythematous vaginal mucosa on exam. 4. Unremarkable urinalysis.
vaginitis.diagnostic_basis: 1. The predominant vaginal symptoms, itching and discharge, with external-only dysuria and a normal urinalysis point to a vaginal rather than urinary source of the discomfort. 2. Wet mount findings confirm the vaginal infectious etiology.
vaginitis.differential_process: 1. Urinary tract infection and cystitis are excluded by a normal urinalysis without pyuria, nitrites, or bacteriuria, and by the absence of true urinary frequency or urgency. 2. Urethritis is considered but the discharge and itching localize to the vagina rather than the urethral meatus, and there is no history of a new sexual partner suggesting a urethral sexually transmitted infection.

urethritis.medical_history: 1. A 24-year-old male reports urethral discharge and burning with urination for four days, following a new sexual partner two weeks ago. 2. No perineal pain or fever.
urethritis.physical_examination: 1. Mucopurulent discharge expressed from the urethral meatus.
urethritis.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urethral swab NAAT positive for Chlamydia trachomatis. 2. Urine culture negative.
urethritis.clinical_features: 1. Urethral discharge and dysuria following a new sexual exposure. 2. Mucopurulent discharge on examination. 3. Positive NAAT for a sexually transmitted pathogen with a negative urine culture.
urethritis.diagnostic_basis: 1. Mucopurulent urethral discharge with dysuria following a recent new sexual partner, a positive NAAT for Chlamydia trachomatis, and a negative standard urine culture are characteristic of infectious urethritis rather than a typical bacterial urinary tract infection.
urethritis.differential_process: 1. Urinary tract infection and cystitis are excluded because the standard urine culture is negative, and the discharge localizes to the urethral meatus rather than reflecting bladder-source symptoms. 2. Prostatitis is considered given the patient is male, but the absence of perineal pain, fever, or a tender/boggy prostate on exam argues against it. 3. Vaginitis is not applicable in a male patient.

prostatitis.medical_history: 1. A 38-year-old male reports acute perineal pain, fever, chills, and dysuria for two days. 2. Associated urinary frequency and a weak stream.
prostatitis.physical_examination: 1. Digital rectal examination reveals a tender, warm, boggy prostate, performed gently given suspected acute infection.
prostatitis.auxiliary_examination: 1. Urinalysis shows pyuria and bacteriuria. 2. Urine culture grows E. coli. 3. Mild leukocytosis on complete blood count.
prostatitis.clinical_features: 1. Acute perineal pain with fever and chills. 2. Tender, boggy prostate on exam. 3. Pyuria, bacteriuria, and leukocytosis. 4. Associated dysuria and weak stream.
prostatitis.diagnostic_basis: 1. The acute onset of perineal pain, fever, and chills, combined with a tender, boggy prostate on examination and a positive urine culture, is characteristic of acute bacterial prostatitis.
prostatitis.differential_process: 1. Urethritis and urinary tract infection are considered as related or contributing processes but the systemic signs, fever and chills, and prostatic tenderness localize the primary infection to the prostate. 2. Hyperplasia of prostate is excluded by the acute, febrile presentation rather than a slowly progressive obstructive course. 3. Carcinoma of prostate is excluded by the acute infective presentation and a soft, boggy, not hard or nodular, gland on exam.
